serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 80 g, roughly 3–4 small kuih pieces)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Kuih Cara Berlauk is approximately 80 grams, equivalent to 3 to 4 small boat-shaped or flower-shaped kuih pieces, as commonly served at Malay tea-time or breakfast spreads.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Foods — HPB Singapore / IMR Malaysia MyFCD composite estimate for traditional Malay savoury kuih
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Kuih Cara Berlauk exists in the HPB or IMR MyFCD databases. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest analogous traditional Malay savoury kuih entries (wheat flour and coconut milk batter base with spiced meat topping) in the HPB Singapore food composition database and IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Input AI-generated values were cross-checked against these sources and retained where consistent. Saturated fat estimated from coconut milk contribution (~4.5 g per 100 g) based on HPB coconut milk composition data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Wheat Flour-Based Snacks — Analogous Estimate from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Kuih Cara Berlauk exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 60 (medium) is estimated by analogy with wheat flour-based steamed or mold-cooked snacks with coconut milk, which typically fall in the GI range of 55–65. The protein from minced meat and eggs and the fat from coconut milk moderate the glycemic response relative to plain wheat flour products. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 16.4) / 100 ≈ 9.8, where carbohydrate per 80 g serving = 20.5 × 0.8 = 16.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content of 280 mg per 100 g; a standard 80 g serving contributes approximately 224 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one or two pieces and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods, as the cumulative sodium from spiced meat seasoning and added salt can approach a meaningful fraction of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour base with a medium glycemic index of approximately 60 and a glycemic load of ~9.8 per 80 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit intake to one or two small pieces per occasion, monitor total carbohydrate load (approximately 16 g per 80 g serving), and pair with a low-GI food or additional protein to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (estimated ~4.5 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Kuih Cara Berlauk in moderation, as the saturated fat from coconut milk may contribute to LDL cholesterol elevation; limiting intake to one serving and choosing lean minced chicken over beef can reduce saturated fat exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kuih Cara Berlauk is a traditional Malay savoury snack consisting of small, boat-shaped or flower-shaped cakes cooked in a specialized brass or cast-iron mold called an acuan. The batter is made from wheat flour, coconut milk, and eggs, producing a soft, slightly rich cake base. Unlike its sweet counterpart Kuih Cara Manis, this savoury version is topped with a spiced minced meat mixture — typically beef or chicken — combined with chopped fresh chilies and fried shallots, delivering a fragrant, umami-rich flavour profile. The cakes are cooked on a stovetop until set and lightly golden on the base while remaining soft and moist inside. Kuih Cara Berlauk is a popular choice for Malay tea-time (waktu petang), festive breakfast spreads, and traditional kenduri (community feasts). Nutritionally, each 80 g serving provides approximately 168 kcal, 5.2 g protein, 9.0 g fat, and 16.4 g carbohydrate. The coconut milk contributes saturated fat (~3.6 g per serving), while the spiced meat topping adds protein and micronutrients including iron and vitamin B12. Sodium content is moderate at approximately 224 mg per serving. The medium glycemic index (~60) means blood glucose impact is moderate, making portion control advisable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk. The dish is naturally halal and dairy-free but contains gluten from wheat flour and eggs as allergens.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Kuih Cara Berlauk exists in the HPB Singapore or IMR Malaysia MyFCD databases. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest analogous traditional Malay savoury kuih entries and cross-checked against the AI-generated input values, which were internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using comparable wheat flour and coconut milk-based snacks. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for Kuih Cara Berlauk are not available in the HPB Singapore food composition database or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2024; no approved source reports these values for this specific traditional preparation. Saturated fat of 4.5 g per 100 g is estimated from the coconut milk contribution based on HPB coconut milk composition data and is documented as an adapted value. All core macros (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a wheat flour, coconut milk, and spiced meat-based snack; no zero-macro justification is required. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement data for this food are not available in any approved GI source. Tamil and Hindi local names are not documented in available approved sources for this Malaysian Malay dish and have been left null; this dish is not classified as a Singaporean dish requiring mandatory Tamil and Hindi coverage under the multilingual rules.
